metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Substack deliberately prevents new newsletters from being indexed on Google, hiding them from search engines. Checking a Substack's own sitemap confirms the problem: the posts are not being surfaced to crawlers. Forty-five articles about AI, education, and computational skepticism can remain invisible to every search engine on the planet. Any fix must preserve inline formatting and structure, including headers, lists, links, and images, when republishing content.

artifactLines: Substack deliberately prevents new newsletters from being indexed on Google, hiding them from search engines.
Checking a Substack's own sitemap confirms the problem: the posts are not being surfaced to crawlers.
Forty-five articles about AI, education, and computational skepticism can remain invisible to every search engine on the planet.
Any fix must preserve inline formatting and structure, including headers, lists, links, and images, when republishing content.

beats.narration_text: Learning that Substack deliberately blocks new newsletters from Google indexing, leaving forty-five articles invisible, and that checking my own sitemap confirms it, makes me want a concrete escape route. I want to build one that preserves bold, italic, strikethrough, headers, lists, links, and images. Can you (1) explain what a sitemap check actually reveals about indexing, (2) outline a mirror or canonical-content setup that gets the same posts crawled, and (3) give me a step-by-step way to verify a single article finally appears in search?
